summary:
John Calvin (1509–1564 AD) was the second-generation reformer whose systematic theology and the theocratic Geneva he created transformed the [[event:102|Protestant Reformation]] from a German regional phenomenon into an international movement — Calvinism became the dominant form of Protestantism in Switzerland, France (Huguenots), Scotland (Presbyterianism), the Netherlands (Dutch Reformed Church), and profoundly influenced Puritanism in England and New England. Calvin, a French lawyer turned theologian who had fled France after his conversion to Protestantism, arrived in Geneva in 1536 and (after an initial expulsion) returned in 1541 to govern the city as a theocracy of extraordinary strictness for the next 23 years. His Institutes of the Christian Religion (1536, expanded through multiple editions) was the most systematically complete theological work of the Reformation.

Calvin's distinctive theological contributions centered on predestination (the doctrine that God has eternally elected some people for salvation and others for damnation, regardless of human merit or effort) and the absolute sovereignty of God. His Geneva was a reforming city of moral discipline: the consistory (church council) monitored behavior, theater was restricted, dancing was prohibited, and heretics were occasionally executed (most famously Michael Servetus, burned for denying the Trinity in 1553 — an act Calvin approved). Geneva became a 'school of Christ' for Protestant refugees from across Europe, who returned to their home countries carrying Calvinist theology and organizational models. The Presbyterian church structure Calvin developed (governance by elected elders rather than bishops) proved compatible with anti-monarchical politics, contributing to the [[event:2327|English Civil War]] and [[event:2406|Dutch Revolt]]. Max Weber's famous thesis linked Calvinist theology (the search for signs of election in worldly success) to the origins of capitalist culture — though Weber's argument remains contested.
